Your Voice, Your Power. 

Navigating mental health advocacy can be daunting, especially when doing it alone, but now you don’t have to. Our Your Voice is Your Power Resource Hub (YVYP Hub) is better than ever. Leveraging self, peer, and community-style resourcing as the foundation for digital wellness, youth and young adults are better equipped than ever to build resilience and sustain their well-being.

Resilience begins with pursuing education, building connection, and living in action. The YVYP Hub will be your one stop shop with all your needs as a beginner mental health advocate. 

Your mental health advocacy journey begins today and is informed by the YVYP Hub. 

Visit the link in our bio 🔗

posts.caption:
With Mental Health Awareness Month around the corner, we’re kicking things off by reintroducing ourselves. We’re Active Minds, and we’re championing a new era of mental health. 🧠💪 

Swipe to learn more about who we are and what we do, and and stay tuned for a closer look at our programs, impact, and how you can become a mental health champion this May and beyond. 

#MentalHealthAwarenessMonth #NewEraOfMentalHealth

Training Alert! 

From student loans to mental health, your everyday choices and life changing decisions are impacted by federal policy. You deserve the agency to understand how. 

Join Active Minds and The Hope Center for Student Basic Needs on Tuesday, April 28th at 6:30 to learn about how federal policy impacts YOUth. 

Visit the link in our bio to register TODAY

Long weekend = long self-care 
Which self care starter pack are you choosing?

Feel like you don’t know how to support your friends? You don’t need to be an expert, just A.S.K. — Acknowledge, Support, Keep-in-Touch.

This #MentalHealthActionDay, we’re excited to share with you the A.S.K. digital experience made in collaboration with @mtv.

In this YouTube digital experience, you’ll guide a simulated conversation between two friends. You will see the direct impact of your words, and better understand emotional support.

You can’t always change what someone is going through, but you can Acknowledge their feelings, Support by listening, and Keep-in-Touch regularly to let them know how much you care. 

Visit our YouTube and start putting A.S.K. into action! #AskToHelp

posts.caption:
April is #AutismAwarenessMonth and today is #WorldAutismAwarenessDay. ♥️ 

Autism is a way some people’s brains work differently. Autistic people may prefer routines, focus deeply on their interests, and see the world in unique ways. These differences should be accepted and supported, not “fixed.” Behaviors like masking, or feeling pressured to behave neurotypically, can lead to autistic burnout and added stress. 

In a world that was designed for neurotypical brains, we're here to remind you that you're allowed to prioritize your mental health, too. Your well-being matters, and you are allowed to do what you need to do to stay mentally well. 

Learn more about autism and mental health at the 🔗 in our bio.

posts.caption:
#MentalHealthMonth is ONE MONTH AWAY! 💚

Since founding Mental Health Month (also referred to as Mental Health Awareness Month) in 1949, Mental Health America has led the effort every May to promote mental wellness nationwide.

Today, we’re thrilled to announce the release of our 2026 Mental Health Month Action Guide, a roadmap for supporting more good days, both individually and together. We know that “good” means something different for everyone. That’s why this year, we’ve created a guide that meets you wherever you are, on the good and the not-so-good days.

Our Mental Health Month merch is also available now! Stock up and be seen in green this May.

Don’t forget to join us TOMORROW, April 2, at 1 pm ET for our virtual Mental Health Month Kickoff event! Whether you’re an educator, advocate, fundraiser, or just hoping to improve your own mental health, this event will help you get ready for May.

Stay up to date with all things #MentalHealthMonth at the 🔗 in our bio. 💚

posts.caption:
CW: sexual assault, sexual abuse

April is Sexual Assault Awareness and Prevention Month (#SAAPM). Sexual violence is a deeply traumatic experience, and trauma has a long-lasting effect on our mental health. People who have been abused are at risk for developing depression, PTSD, substance use disorders, anxiety, and eating disorders.

If you have experienced sexual violence, remember that abuse is never your fault. Recovering from abuse is difficult, and the process is nonlinear, but it IS possible. If you’re a survivor, these messages are for you. 🩵

Find more resources at the 🔗 in our bio. 

For immediate support, reach out to:
@RAINN 24/7 hotline: 1-800-656-7673 
National Domestic Violence Hotline (@NDVHofficial): 1-800-7988-7233

If you or someone you know is in crisis, call or text 988 or chat 988lifeline.org (@988lifeline).

posts.caption:
It’s normal to feel overwhelmed by big problems in the world around you, especially when your community is facing violence or discrimination. These feelings are difficult, but you don’t have to cope with them by yourself.

If current events are impacting your mental health, you are not alone. Mental Health America’s Crisis Resource Center has information and tools designed to help you through difficult moments, like violence, discrimination, identity-based hate, or global conflict. Visit the 🔗 in our bio to learn more.

If you’re struggling and need immediate support, help is available. Crisis Text Line provides free, 24/7, confidential mental health support in English and Spanish. Text HELLO to 741741 to be connected with a Crisis Counselor today.

Mental health is health. 🌎

On #WorldHealthDay and every day, Mental Health America is committed to promoting mental health as a critical part of our well-being.

Physical and mental health are equally important and deeply connected. Things like sleep, stress, movement, nutrition, and existing health conditions all play a role in how we feel, both mentally and physically. 

Learn more about the connection between our physical and mental health at the 🔗 in our bio. 💙

posts.caption:
If news about wars, humanitarian crises, or global conflict has been affecting your mental health, know that you are not alone.

According to World Psychiatry, the mental health of the civilian population is one of the most significant impacts of war and major conflict. Whether you’re directly or indirectly affected by a crisis, your feelings are valid.

Mental Health America has resources to help you cope with global conflicts, like war, terrorism, geopolitical tension, territorial disputes, and political instability. 

Visit the 🔗 in our bio to find the support you need.

💪🏿💪🏽💪🏼 All couples fight. 💪🏿💪🏽💪🏼 ⁠
⁠
Ironically, it's one of the most important parts of relationship. And couples that fight "well" -- in a way that resolves conflict rather than perpetuates it -- are more likely to stay together happily. ⁠
⁠
The same is true for other relationships in our life, too.⁠
⁠
But we're rarely taught that fighting is good, right? Rather it's something that we want to avoid, not practice.⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
Big NEWS!!! My new book, The Balancing Act, is available now for preorder. 

Going beyond buzzwords like “codependency” and “emotional immaturity,” I encourage us to take a closer look at ourselves in our lives. How can we form connections without losing our individuality? How can we set boundaries in certain situations while remaining open in others? 

The Balancing Act explores how our relationship styles are influenced by introversion and extroversion, our attachment styles, and how our family systems shape how we engage with others. Regardless of how we arrived at our current state, I guide us toward improvement, deeper relationships, and the honoring of our needs.

The Balancing Act serves as a guide to achieving harmony in our relationships, steering us away from the extremes of dependency. Every relationship—whether with a partner, friend, or family member—requires a delicate balance. When we overextend ourselves, we risk losing our sense of self. Conversely, when we withdraw too much, we jeopardize our connections. The key is to find a middle ground where both authenticity and closeness can flourish.

I’m excited for you to read The Balancing Act,	out February 10th and available for preorder now!
I’m deeply grateful for your support and forever thankful for how my career as a therapist transformed me into an author. 

Thank you to my team @tarcher_books and my agent @presentperfectlit 

#thebalancingact

posts.caption:
life has been… full. 🦋

behind the scenes, we’ve been thinking deeply about where @selfcareisforeveryone is going… how we want to keep showing up… and how to build it up from here so that it supports people in real & needed ways.

and in the middle of all of that, we’ve also just been… human.

a little overwhelmed at times.
a lot in our heads.
doing our best with what’s in front of us.

maybe you’re feeling this too in your own life.

so if you’re in a season where you’re carrying more than usual mentally, emotionally, or just generally in life, here’s something we’ve reminding ourselves of over and over:

🍄 it’s okay to do less when you’re dealing with more. 🐸

you don’t have to keep up all the time.
you don’t have to push through everything.
you don’t have to be at your “best” to still be worthy of care.

checking in and taking care of yourself is ALWAYS a priority. plus… everything else tends to work out even better than you can imagine when you’re pouring from a fuller cup 🪄✨

posts.caption:
Your anxiety is lying to you.
And the worst part? It’s convincing.

Anxiety doesn’t just make you feel scared — it makes you believe the fear is fact.

It whispers that you’re not capable. That you’ll fail. That it’s safer to stay small.

But here’s what I know after years of working with anxious women: the things anxiety tells you that you CAN’T do are usually the exact things you need to do most.

The job application. The hard conversation. The trip alone. The boundary. The dream.

Anxiety lies. Your potential doesn’t.

Every time you do the thing anyway — even shaking, even scared — you’re rewriting the story your nervous system has been telling you.
That’s not bravery. That’s healing.

💬 What’s one thing your anxiety has tried to talk you out of? Drop it in the comments — let’s call it out together.

posts.caption:
I’m afraid to admit that:

1. Im 45 and I still have never been married.
At this age, that can feel loud. Not because marriage defines success, but because the world treats it like it does. Sometimes I feel behind. Saying it out loud helps me separate shame from truth. A different timeline does not mean a lesser life.
	
2. I’ve never been engaged.
No proposal story. No ring. And yes, sometimes that stings. It is not about the diamond. It is about feeling chosen. Admitting that longing makes it softer. Wanting partnership is not weakness.
	
3. I’ve never lived with a man.
I have built my life on my own and I am proud of that. But I have also wondered what it would feel like to share it. Being independent and craving connection can exist at the same time.
	
4. I’m not sure if I’ve ever truly been in love.
I have cared deeply. I have felt attachment. But the steady, safe kind of love? I am not sure I have had that yet. Admitting this helps me look honestly at my patterns and stay open to something healthier.
	
5. I’ve never wanted kids.
For years I questioned myself. I worried something was wrong with me. It took time to trust that my desires are valid. Owning that truth set me free.
	
6. I still struggle with anxiety and depression sometimes.
Even after years of healing. Even as a therapist. Healing is layered, not linear. Old waves still roll in. Admitting that keeps me human and honest. It does not cancel my growth.
	
7. I’m scared of ending up alone.
This one is the hardest. I love my life. I love my independence. And sometimes I still feel that fear. Saying it out loud takes away some of its power.

Admitting what we are afraid to say builds self trust. Shame grows in silence. Honesty creates connection.

Admitting what we are afraid to say builds self trust. Shame grows in silence. Honesty creates connection.

💛 Alison @theanxietyhealer #livinglifemyway

Would it be great to have someone to share it all with? 10 years ago I would’ve said “yes, absolutely.” Now? Not sure. I know for sure though that I’m enough.

No wedding photos.
No house in my name.

And for a long time, I thought that meant I was behind.

But here’s what no one tells women over 40:

The timeline panic isn’t biological.
It’s cultural.

We were raised to believe:
Marriage = security.
Children = fulfillment.
Homeownership = stability.
Aging = decline.

But what if fulfillment looks different?

👉What if peace is the milestone?
👉What if freedom is the achievement?
👉What if self-trust is the glow-up?

I’m not rejecting love.
I’m rejecting pressure.

And that changes everything.

If you’re a woman over 40 navigating identity shifts, timelines, or societal expectations…you’re not behind.

You’re evolving. ❤️

posts.caption:
Emotions carry important information about what you value, what hurts, what needs attention. But information isn’t instruction. 

The clinical psychologist Harriet Lerner says it best: “Feelings are like children. You don’t want them driving the car, but you shouldn’t stuff them in the trunk either.” 

They belong in the passenger seat. You hear them out; maybe they even change your route. But your values are driving. What do you want your life to look like in five years? What kind of person are you trying to be? 

Those questions should steer, not whether you woke up feeling motivated.

Keep reading this article at www.thepreamble.com I write for Sharon McMahon every week at the preamble.

posts.caption:
I almost didn't dig into this research because it felt wrong. I've screamed in my car and definitely recommended it to clients.  It feels good so I didn't want to find out it wasn't actually doing anything.

But when I finally looked at the studies, it clicked. That "relief" I was feeling wasn't because I released something. It was because I revved myself up so high that coming back down felt like progress. I was manufacturing a crash and calling it catharsis.

This doesn't mean coping skills are useless or that you should never let yourself feel angry. Feel your anger. The problem is when we confuse discharging an emotion with processing it. 

Screaming into a pillow, smashing things in a rage room, punching a bag while thinking about the person who pissed you off — the research consistently shows it makes you more aggressive afterward, not less.

Sam @sam.the.therapistt and I go deep on this in this week's episode of Nuance Needed 106

We get into: the history of where this idea came from (a therapist in the 1960s who claimed screaming could cure cancer, I wish I was kidding), what the research actually says, and what works instead. Link in bio.

embedded_comments.text: Omg did we talk about this that it would make me just more angry? 😂 @taylorstronge

I think I would argue that I’m totally fine with being more aggressive after doing somatic work where I let myself express my anger. It actually gives me more energy to stick up for myself. Women need to be more aggressive, our fight instinct has been shoved so far down into fawning. The study doesn’t convince me I should stop, it actually convinces me that it’s helping me unlock healthy fight instincts to stick up for myself rather than freezing or fawning.

I’ve thought about this for years when friends invited me to rage rooms in the past. I get that it could be fun, bonding, and release some anger. I live from the perspective that I refuse to become violent like those who harmed me, even if it’s controlled and even if it’s inanimate objects meant to be broken; I strongly feel that conveying any type of violence would beget further violence. It can enable it; it could become a new way of coping. I worry about its effects. It’s something I refuse to do to myself.

I love talking with clients about how to express anger (which may including throwing things) but how to do it mindfully and with intention- a loss of control is not the goal. And a crucial part is to transition into a controlled activity. Example- I use a hammer to smash pottery I didn’t like and I use each smash to express a frustration. Then I immediately reclaim the clay to make something new.

There’s a difference between having a strategy if and when anger arises - eg “punch the pillow instead of your brother” and eliciting or exciting rage as catharsis.  In mindfulness integrated  CBT Bruno Cayoun talks about how when he practiced crying he then created a strong neural pathway for crying which he couldn’t then switch off.  Transmuting anger is a far more powerful  and effective tool for catharsis - to learn how to feel it without shame and to move through it without causing damage.  The we have a chance to harness the power behind the anger for constructive change.

This is exactly it. 
I think it’s because Catharsis is being sold as a “cure all”.
It doesn’t “fix” anything.

Catharsis temporarily clears stuck emotion. If you do nothing with that clarity, then the actions and habits which cause the emotion to build up and get stuck will just perpetuate. 

So, instead, use the clarity gained from catharsis to make *actionable changes* in your life. Transform the pattern.

posts.caption:
“I don’t owe anyone anything.” Until someone doesn’t owe you anything either.

Imagine you haven’t responded to a friend’s text in three days. You’ve been busy. You don’t owe anyone your time.

Now imagine that same friend doesn’t respond to you for three days. Notice how fast your brain starts writing a story about what it means.

We’ve gotten really comfortable saying “I don’t owe anyone anything.” But we’re not as honest about how it feels when someone says it back.

The rules you set for yourself apply to everyone else, too. That’s the part we keep forgetting.
•
•
•
For more conversations that bring nuance back to the internet, listen to my podcast, Nuance Needed, and follow @therapyforwomen

embedded_comments.text: I tell my friends, you have no moral obligation to respond to my text message or call ever.  No justification or judgment.  And I’ve absolutely maintained healthy and loving friendships with understanding and respect.

Not texting back for 3 days is one thing, but I've had people not text back for months. At some point you have to take a lack of reply as a silent message they don't want to be your friend anymore.
Increased capacity for other’s emotions and behaviors is the answer! Cutting folks off because you have no capacity for any negative energy is NOT.

As someone with ADHD whose symptoms involve being terrible at texting, what I owe people is that I'm terrible at texting and that my response rate will be extremely poor. They can either communicate with me by calling or on Discord if they want more regular responses. I stopped assuming the worst of people a long time ago. I am continually receiving the grace I give to others, and I am so grateful for that.
I actually think we owe each other a lot — but it’s not supposed to feel like a debt or obligation when you love someone. Humans are wired to be community centered.

I've experienced "I've never felt like this before" chaotic NRE that was unhealthy but I also needed to know how it felt so that I could learn to not do that to myself again, and it showed me the "I've never felt like this before" calm and steady and exciting and fulfilling NRE was actually safe and what I wanted.

I discovered limerence when I stepped in it. I had such an intense attraction to someone that I actually told her that she “lights me up”. I also had an anxiety attack that I never experienced before! So now I know about anxious/avoidant attachment and  CPTSD and lots of other modern relationship stuff!

I definitely said it too early (thank goodness it didn't scare him off LOL) but I really did mean it in a safe way. Even early on I felt so at peace with him, and my usual relationship anxieties were so easy to work on *because* he's been transparent about his feelings and boundaries from the start and he reassures me if I ever ask for it. Coming from my last relationship that felt like walking on egg shells, yeah, I really haven't felt this happy and secure about anyone before.
My love for my partner felt calm, almost homey, that’s one of the reasons I knew I wanted to stay with him ❤️
Hmm. Ok but what if I haven't felt this before 😄 The combination of safety + calm + genuine care paired with intensity and passion is scary, exciting, wonderful, beautiful all at once.

During the last phase of a very unhappy marriage, I experienced a “year of limerence” obsessing over a poet-friend who lived 8,000 miles away. We met in a Zoom poetry group. He lived in Thailand. It was a thrilling time; I deepened my friendship with the poet, was productive, felt powerful and beautiful, was so happy. Meanwhile my marriage was in shambles and, coincidentally the poet suddenly died, I crashed, absolutely fell apart. But then with some understanding friends as my anchors, I began the real work of being in therapy and then entering into divorce proceedings. Through all of it, I do not regret that crazy year of obsession and delight. I learned so much, produced some of my best poetry, and came out a much more self-aware person.

I feel like its „normal” to feel that way in the beggining, when you are head over heels - even if you did feel that way before you can’t remember it in the moment and it feels like the first time. Not always a bad thing, can be cute as long as you dont start to despsise that person after the honeymoon phase…
I believe this clip really helped me, thank you for sharing and doing gods work. I bought your book about a year ago btw❤️

This is ridiculous. You are giving examples of a few minor incidents, ones that parents would regret, for sure, but they do not count as abuse and would not ruin a child's sense of safety or make them emotionally insecure. The parent who REPEATEDLY forgets to pick up a child from school (or shows up drunk or stoned), the parent who REPEATEDLY loses his temper or who NEVER shows love and affection would definitely leave emotional scars on the child. But you are conflating real abuse with common mistakes that normal parents make, and that's grossly misleading. You are also minimizing the resilience of the young child, and framing the child as a victim.

cause it’s one thing to make a mistake here and there, but to create a pattern of inconsistency and dysregulation which you then project, all of that instability onto your own biological innocent child is diabolical. and when you become an adult after going through that as a child, giving yourself  the space to acknowledge that it’s actually healthy to not want to be around people that make you feel unsafe -owning that is such a freeing feeling.  it’s one thing if it was a few mistakes here and there but from what I experienced, it was professionally and clinically, abusive, and neglectful. and being estranged is something I would never wish on anybody, but its something i can now finally accept . As you read these, we might find that we gravitate to the same cognitive distortions. ⁠
⁠
We may tend to be self-blaming or other-blaming, tend to fixate on small details or extrapolate to extremes. ⁠
⁠
Understanding what cognitive distortions our minds favor can help you begin to unpack a lot of the fundamental beliefs that we may hold about ourselves that aren't true, and slowly learn to unlearn and heal. ⁠
⁠
